--- SOURCE 2: Evaluating the Impact of Augmented and Virtual Reality ---
URL: https://www.aam-us.org/2021/01/15/evaluating-the-impact-of-augmented-and-virtual-reality/

SUMMARY:
We see AR and VR as tools that will greatly influence the future of our museum—and probably yours, too.

FULL CONTENT:
![](https://www.facebook.com/tr?id=1282009681853288&ev=PageView&noscript=1)

# Evaluating the Impact of Augmented and Virtual Reality

![Children using a VR headset and AR app on an iPad inside a museum](https://www.aam-us.org/wp-content/uploads/2021/01/Untitled-design-24.png?resize=1280%2C720)

## Share this article

“I am perfectly astonished at the effect.”

“The thrilling scenes therein depicted are beyond description.”

“It is true and really wonderful.”

These are reactions from visitors to the Chicago Fire Cyclorama, who, in 1892, were captivated by the sensation of standing in the center of a rotunda, surrounded on all sides by a detailed [painting](https://www.facebook.com/chicago00.org/photos/a.2396056237310012/2396056410643328/?type=3) of a burning city. Perched atop slowly spinning platforms, audiences got a 360-degree view and a narrated tour of the already historic 1871 Chicago Fire. Two tons of oil paint, six tons of canvas, and twenty person-years of labor resulted in scenes so massive and so realistically rendered that crowds delighted at the feeling of being transported back in time. Cycloramas were as close to virtual reality as you could get in 1892, and the novelty left an impression.

Apart from the quaint turns of phrase, the sentiments from the Chicago Fire Cyclorama visitors are strikingly similar to those offered by recent users of the Chicago History Museum’s virtual and augmented reality project, [Chicago00](https://www.chicago00.org/) (“Chicago zero zero”), which we developed as a new kind of museum exhibition: mobile, immersive, and driven by the choices of the user. The project elevates our mission to share Chicago’s stories by activating them on the digital landscape.

“It felt like I could reach out and touch everything around me.”

“The experience took me back in time.”

“I loved it, magnificent view into what it was like back then.”

Like the creators of cycloramas, the team behind CHM’s experimental digital history initiative aims to astonish. Only instead of tons of paint and twenty thousand square feet of canvas, we leverage new technology, images from CHM’s extensive Prints and Photographs Collection, and other media archives to connect users with pivotal events in Chicago’s past. Through augmented reality apps and virtual reality videos, the project superimposes historical imagery onto the contemporary landscape. Stunning visuals accompany ambient sound, music, and narration to accomplish immersive storytelling that grounds users in the history all around them. At the edge of the Chicago River between Clark and LaSalle Streets, for example, smartphones become portals to the past, resurrecting ghostly scenes from when the S.S. Eastland capsized on that very spot over a century ago. The [AR](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PKJLbi-vzIc) is a moving experience for users, inducing gasps and exclamations that satisfy both the historians and the creative team behind the experience.

![A panoramic photograph of a cityscape behind a lake, with black-and-white historical photographs superimposed on matching areas.](https://www.aam-us.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/ManufacturersRoof_OverlayComposite_mono-scaled-1.jpg?resize=1024%2C246)

But Chicago00 was always supposed to be more than a spectacle. It was meant to take the simulated reality that has been delighting gamers and amusement park goers for years and apply it to learning and connecting with history in new ways. As a history museum, our goal from the outset was to explore digital storytelling as a means to deliver on our mission. Our partnership with filmmaker and professor Geoffrey Alan Rhodes, which began in 2013, has pushed us to take the stories at our fingertips—the 1929 St. Valentine’s Day Massacre, the 1933 Century of Progress World’s Fair, the 1968 Democratic National Convention protests—and revitalize them with new interpretation and innovative presentation for an expanded audience. Designed as a series of experiments, each 00 initiative has been an evolution, allowing us to test, iterate, and refine. When the project was honored with a MUSE award from AAM in 2018, CHM and our partners were thrilled with how far we had come and how much we had learned.

## Related Stories

### [Me/We/Here/There: museums and the matrix of place-based augmented devices](https://www.aam-us.org/2016/05/01/meweherethere-museums-and-the-matrix-of-place-based-augmented-devices/)

Category: 
[Center for the Future of Museums Blog](https://www.aam-us.org/category/future-of-museums/)

### [Is Augmented Reality the Ultimate Museum App: Some Strategic Considerations](https://www.aam-us.org/2011/09/01/is-augmented-reality-the-ultimate-museum-app-some-strategic-considerations/)

Category: 
[Museum Magazine](https://www.aam-us.org/category/museum-magazine/)

### [The Connected Museum of 2040](https://www.aam-us.org/2017/11/16/the-connected-museum-of-2040/)

Category: 
[Center for the Future of Museums Blog](https://www.aam-us.org/category/future-of-museums/)

### [Outside the Collection Box: Connecting community with collections via augmented reality](https://www.aam-us.org/2019/04/05/outside-the-collection-box-connecting-community-with-collections-via-augmented-reality/)

Category: 
[Alliance Blog](https://www.aam-us.org/category/alliance-labs/)

The only thing was, even as we watched the people engaging with our VR and AR experiences gasp, we could not tell you for certain if they were learning anything about history. Museum AR and VR is relatively new, and very little has been developed and shared on evaluating the effectiveness of these formats in achieving experience goals for audiences. Analytics tell a nebulous story of views, clicks, and likes, but it’s difficult to draw meaningful conclusions from these numbers. According to museum evaluator Kate Haley Goldman, “Museums lack tools, scales, and even a vocabulary for measuring immersion, presence, and empathy, and how those elements might contribute to learning outcomes.” With the support of a National Endowment for the Humanities Digital Projects for the Public grant, CHM set out to change that. We used the development of our latest VR experience, *Chicago00: 1893 World’s Columbian Exposition,* to create and test an evaluation tool to measure the cognitive and affective impact of these emerging digital experiences.

## Our Process

Working alongside Haley Goldman and her team at HG&Co, we embarked on a process to help us understand how users engage with digital history experiences, what impressions they had of our experience, and what was sinking in, with the goal of integrating this user testing into future design development.

As a test case, HG&Co evaluators observed about fifty participants trying out a previous Chicago00 VR project, [*Century of Progress*](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=b2QPWTZ7bwQ), a vivid portrayal of the 1933 World’s Fair that culminates with a simulated trip up the 628-foot Sky Ride to get a bird’s eye view of the city. Afterwards, they interviewed the participants—who included students on a field trip, museum educators, and general museum visitors—about the experience.

![Participants wearing VR headsets in a room with screens reading "Chicago 00: 1893 World's Columbian Exposition"](https://www.aam-us.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/IMG_0503-scaled-1.jpeg?resize=392%2C294)

While participants were inside the headset, the evaluators observed them pointing, exclaiming, and even grimacing, as they were brought high up in the Sky Ride, then cajoling friends and family members to try it out next. The wow factor was in full effect. The ensuing interviews confirmed the experience achieved powerful sensory immersion for the participants—so much so that, in some cases, they had trouble retaining or reflecting on the ideas being communicated. At times, they even actively tuned out the audio content in order to focus on being “there.” We learned that multiple viewings were warranted to fully grasp the historical information, which supported our approach to make the experiences free, easily accessible, and appealing to return to.

The disconnect between the sensory elements and the narration was a key finding for us. We also discovered that:

These findings—some surprising and some expected—became a formative study for our new World’s Columbian Exposition VR. We considered the complexity and pacing of the narration, the then-and-now images working together to say “history happened here,” and the drama of the moving Ferris wheel. Realizing that the experience would prompt curiosity about the 1893 fair, we developed a [web portal](https://1893.chicago00.org/) with mapped imagery and interpretive content. Available now, [*Chicago 00: 1893 World’s Columbian Exposition*](https://wce.chicago00.org/) is also the first of our digital history projects to be thoroughly evaluated with our new Experience Evaluation Rubric.

## The Experience Evaluation Rubric (EER)

Based on the user testing for *Century of Progress*, HG&Co developed a [framework for impact measurement for museum VR/AR](https://chicago00.org/Chicago00_OurProcess_October_2020.pdf) called the Experience Evaluation Rubric (EER). The EER was designed to build a shared vocabulary and specificity around mixed reality experiences. At CHM, we use it to assess and inform the episodes we create of historical events in specific physical locations, but the tool is easily applied to other types of museum-based content and a range of experience goals.

The EER has two sections. The first is for the designer or evaluator to complete, and it captures details about the physical and digital environment of the experience and the content presented. The second is a participant-facing assessment that asks users to score twenty or so statements related to presence factors as well as cognitive and affective impacts. Using a scale of 1 (not at all) to 7 (completely), audiences rate qualities ranging from vividness and captivation to time-travel and physicality of place. These latter two elements were of particular interest to our history-driven, location-based project, but other museums might place more emphasis on traits related to agency or social presence, for example, depending on their intended effects.

Over time, data from the two sections of the EER can be analyzed for emergent patterns, such as what type and context of experience provides what type of impact. Museum digital producers can then make informed decisions about the kinds of content, technology, and settings that best support a desired outcome. For example, after viewing our World’s Columbian Exposition episode, 81 percent of test subjects indicated they wanted to visit the Museum’s World’s Fair exhibition content, which demonstrates VR’s potential to support our on-site audience development goal. We also discovered that 87 percent of test subjects were very interested in the details within the virtual world, which indicates that the design of the experience succeeds at piquing curiosity about historical visuals. Finally, over 80 percent reported learning new facts, affirming the educational effectiveness we sought as we designed the experience.

As the Chicago History Museum works toward its commitment to becoming a digital-first museum, projects like Chicago00 are at the top of [our strategic agenda](https://www.chicagohistory.org/wp-content/uploads/2018/08/DFC-1.0-Report-FINAL-6.21.2018-reduced.pdf). With support from the National Endowment for the Humanities, which encourages new, technology-driven interpretive methods for museums and widespread sharing of resulting products and tools, we have been able to meaningfully advance this goal. We now have evidence of the efficacy and appeal of the mixed-reality work we are developing through the Chicago00 project and a pathway for designing future experiences with our desired outcomes in mind. Our Experience Evaluation Rubric offers clear guidance for how all museums experimenting with augmented and virtual reality can understand user engagement and deliver meaningful content.

Immersive storytelling has come a long way since the peak days of cyclorama exhibitions. Worlds displayed on building-size paintings have shrunk to fit inside screens and headsets, but the potential is bigger and the reach is farther than ever before. We see AR and VR as tools that will greatly influence the future of our museum—and probably yours, too.

1.   [Back to Blog](https://hqsoftwarelab.com/blog/)

How Virtual Reality (VR) Enhances Museum Experience
===================================================

 8 min read Last updated: 4 September 2024[Augmented and Virtual Reality](https://hqsoftwarelab.com/blog/augmented-and-virtual-reality/)

Share:

*   [Facebook](https://www.facebook.com/sharer.php?src=sp&u=https%3A%2F%2Fhqsoftwarelab.com%2Fblog%2Far-vr-museums%2F&title=Virtual%20Reality%20(VR)%20and%20Augmented%20Reality%20(AR)%20for%20Museums&utm_source=share2 "Facebook")
*   [Twitter](https://x.com/intent/post?text=Virtual%20Reality%20(VR)%20and%20Augmented%20Reality%20(AR)%20for%20Museums&url=https%3A%2F%2Fhqsoftwarelab.com%2Fblog%2Far-vr-museums%2F&utm_source=share2 "Twitter")
*   [LinkedIn](https://www.linkedin.com/shareArticle?mini=true&url=https%3A%2F%2Fhqsoftwarelab.com%2Fblog%2Far-vr-museums%2F&title=Virtual%20Reality%20(VR)%20and%20Augmented%20Reality%20(AR)%20for%20Museums&utm_source=share2 "LinkedIn")

[Virtual Reality (VR) and Augmented Reality (AR)](https://hqsoftwarelab.com/blog/how-to-implement-ar-vr-solutions-in-your-industry/) for museums today are not just opportunities to stand out. [Custom AR/VR software](https://hqsoftwarelab.com/solutions/ar-vr-development/) is a perfect way to attract new audiences and increase interest in exhibitions. With the help of ‘virtual exhibitions,’ anyone can visit a museum, even if they are thousands of kilometers away, and admire works of art or unique ancient interiors at any time of the day.

In a modern world flooded with content, static artifacts of antiquity do not seem to be an appealing option for spending a weekend. People want the ‘WOW’ effect everywhere. VR and AR technologies make it possible to achieve such effects. Extended Reality (XR) enables the animation of exhibitions. Additionally, interactive exhibits are currently rare, which attracts a young audience that is always in search of new experiences.

Table of contents:

[How museums use AR](https://hqsoftwarelab.com/blog/ar-vr-museums/#howar)

[How museums use VR](https://hqsoftwarelab.com/blog/ar-vr-museums/#howvr)

[Benefits of AR/VR for museums](https://hqsoftwarelab.com/blog/ar-vr-museums/#benefits)

[Challenges museums face](https://hqsoftwarelab.com/blog/ar-vr-museums/#challenge)

[Future of AR/VR for museums](https://hqsoftwarelab.com/blog/ar-vr-museums/#future)

Augmented Reality is used to add an overlay of data and graphic content to a real-world image. All those dreams about augmented vision, ultra-futuristic contact lenses, and cars with displays on the windscreen are based on current and future features of AR.

And Virtual Reality is a technology capable of building well-thought-out simulations rich in content, visuals, mechanics, interactivity, and so on. VR games are blowing up the market, virtual training solutions are being used across industries from healthcare to manufacturing, and the hardware is getting better and providing more and more features for force feedback and spatial navigation.

According to Bloomberg, the AR/VR market will reach $455 billion by 2030. Compare this to the same stats from 2020, when the market was valued at only $15 billion. Impressive, isn’t it?

![Image 5: book template 1 - How Virtual Reality (VR) Enhances Museum Experience](https://hqsoftwarelab.com/blog/ar-vr-museums/)

The market value of AR/VR by 2030

This tremendous success comes thanks to the ability of AR/VR mobile apps to bring benefits to almost any industry. As you already know, these technologies can interrupt nearly any business.

Today, we’ll take a closer look at one such industry you may not have thought of: museums.

The digital evolution that has permeated so many aspects of our lives has come to museums as well.

Museums understand that visitors today are expecting more memorable experiences and want to gain more from each visit than ever before. A visit to the museum needs to be more engaging and emotive than it was in the past, and this is exactly where AR/VR can be utilized.

> ![Image 7: book template 1 - How Virtual Reality (VR) Enhances Museum Experience](https://hqsoftwarelab.com/blog/ar-vr-museums/)

Let’s review the possibilities for how these technologies can be used.

How Can Museums Use Augmented Reality?
--------------------------------------

AR technology in museums is used to provide visitors with immersive experiences inside the museum. AR can enrich everything the visitor sees and enhance the whole experience.

As a result, visitors:

*   Tend to retain memories about their museum visit for longer;
*   Report increased engagement in the whole experience;
*   Are excited to visit the museum more than once;
*   Are eager to share their experience with a larger audience on social media or by word of mouth.

Here are typical use cases for AR in museums.

### AR museum experiences

Not everything can be displayed in the museum’s exhibits—there are artifacts that no longer exist on Earth.

It’s sad that we don’t have the opportunity to see some of the relics of the past because they have been destroyed or lost forever. Also, there are animals that became extinct long ago and the evidence of their existence has been reduced to bones and fossilized paw prints.

But thanks to modern technologies we can still see them—in AR!

For example, the Muséum national d’Histoire Naturelle in Paris has a Hololens-based exhibition “REVIVRE” that shows extinct animals. In AR, visitors can see animals move just as they would have appeared in their natural habitat and at full size.

![Image 9](https://i.ytimg.com/vi/So_4UzvyPO4/hqdefault.jpg)

### Reimagination of classic art

A one-of-a-kind exhibition in Toronto at the Art Gallery of Ontario, by Alex Mayhew, was called “ReBlink.”

The creator of the exhibit reimagined some classic paintings and added modern meanings to them. Visitors can use their phones or tablets to see the paintings come alive and experience the art in a whole new way.

### Art identification

AR is a versatile tool, and when combined with computer vision and image recognition technologies, it becomes an extremely powerful instrument. With AR, visitors can scan any work of art to detect what it is and learn more about the artist and the piece itself.

Galleries and museums can use AR apps to create comprehensive guides for particular exhibitions or complete galleries. This is exactly what Florence’s Uffizi Gallery did; an unofficial app scans artwork, identifies it, and provides users with an audio guide.

The list of possible applications goes on. But we have one more technology to examine, so let’s move on.

How Can Museums Use Virtual Reality?
------------------------------------

Virtual Reality in museums is used to immerse visitors in a whole new experience, in a completely virtual world. A VR museum experience is a work of art in itself, and museums take advantage of that extensively.

For visitors, it is a:

*   New and entertaining way to learn about art;
*   Whole new and one-of-a-kind experience during a museum visit;
*   Perfect opportunity to introduce children and skeptical adults to art and history.

The benefits of using VR in museums are tremendous; let’s see how museums have been applying it.

### Reinvention of classics in VR

VR is a perfect tool to bring a breath of fresh air into classic art.

London’s Victoria and Albert Museum opened an exhibition that explored Lewis Carroll’s classic fantasy novel, Alice in Wonderland.

The playful Virtual Reality experience brings the Wonderland to life and immerses visitors in a truly interactive virtual world—exactly the one that Alice visited in her adventures.

![Image 10](https://i.ytimg.com/vi/j1maAW2F2Ug/hqdefault.jpg)

### Examining art in detail

Old paintings often carry a lot of secrets, about their meaning, their history, and the artist. In VR, all the known information can be combined in a unique experience that helps visitors understand the piece of art better and maybe even unravel some of its mysteries.

That’s[exactly what the Louvre did](https://www.louvre.fr/en/what-s-on/life-at-the-museum/the-mona-lisa-in-virtual-reality-in-your-own-home). Its VR mobile app “Mona Lisa: Beyond the Glass” puts visitors into a virtual room with the woman DaVinci painted—Lisa Gherardini—as if she was alive right now.

![Image 11: book template 1 - How Virtual Reality (VR) Enhances Museum Experience](https://hqsoftwarelab.com/blog/ar-vr-museums/)

The Mona Lisa inside a VR experience

### Interaction with art

Traditionally, art is something you enjoy by observing. There’s not much opportunity to interact with it—you go to a museum, read captions, listen to the art historian’s lecture, and on average that’s it.

Now, there are VR use cases in museums when visitors can actually interact with the exhibits. In Los Angeles, the Peterson Automotive Museum partnered with Microsoft HoloLens to build a VR experience. Using it, visitors can interact with a classic sports car, the Ford GT40.

All these use cases bring unmatched benefits for museums. Read on to learn more.

Benefits of Using AR/VR in Museums
----------------------------------

AR/VR mobile apps help museums to solve various tasks and to benefit from these modern technologies in many ways.

### Expanding target audience

AR technology in museums as well as VR apps can attract a new audience that wasn’t particularly interested in art and history before.

**5 Reasons to Implement AR in Media and Entertainment**

How do modern businesses use AR to increase their revenues and outperform their industry competitors?

[Download the whitepaper](https://hqsoftwarelab.com/whitepapers/whitepaper/)

![Image 13: book template 1 - How Virtual Reality (VR) Enhances Museum Experience](https://hqsoftwarelab.com/blog/ar-vr-museums/)

AR/VR technologies are themselves an attraction, and when paired with art and interesting exhibitions the effect rises significantly.

### Enriched museum experiences

One of the greatest benefits of using VR in museums is that any exhibit can be turned into an exciting simulation with 3D content, interactive communication with the audience, and more.

### Reaching new levels of interaction with visitors

As already mentioned, AR/VR technologies in museums allow for better interactivity, an important benefit.

The more interactive the experience, the more engaged and entertained users are, and this results in overall museum visits being much more exciting and interesting.

### Creating additional revenue streams

Needless to say, virtual tour guides, VR exhibitions, and AR apps for museums are great tools for boosting revenue.

Create an interactive tour guide and sell it in mobile app stores, build a virtual experience to accompany an exhibition, or turn the whole exhibition into a virtual experience—the opportunities for new revenue streams are endless!

But where there are benefits, there are challenges as well. Let’s take a closer look.

Challenges Museums Face Using AR/VR
-----------------------------------

We have highlighted three main challenges for museums that want to or already apply AR/VR.

### AR/VR solutions can be expensive to build

This is truly a limiting factor for most museums.

For VR use cases in museums, buying headsets for visitors alone is pretty expensive, and we haven’t even started talking about development costs. Depending on the size of the project, technical requirements for the experience, and the amount of immersive content required, the costs can vary from $10,000 to $50,000. They’re limited only by the customer’s imagination.

AR is definitely cheaper—most solutions can be developed for smartphones and tablets that users already have. But museums still have to develop and update the content.

Despite the expense of building AR and VR apps, it is a huge revenue channel for museums that will pay for itself fairly quickly.

### Museums are concerned that AR/VR experiences will replace physical facilities

Some museums worry that if they provide users with VR or AR experiences, they will lose interest in traditional art and exhibits and stop visiting museums at all.

But the goal of utilizing AR/VR apps for museums is exactly the opposite—to attract new visitors, ensure everyone is interested in the exhibit since it has become more interactive, and attract kids and adults who have lost interest in art and history.

To appeal to those who aren’t much interested in museums in the first place, there is also an option—offer remote VR mobile apps and AR solutions to turn their home space into an exhibition.

### Visitors get uncomfortable in simulations

The disconnect between the visitor’s physical body and the virtual world can cause some unpleasant symptoms, such as headache, dizziness and eyestrain.

Yet, with the development of hardware and enhancements in screen resolution and quality, we will probably see these issues diminish.

And all these drawbacks can’t stop immersive experiences from shaping the future of museums.

Future of AR/VR in Museums
--------------------------

AR mobile apps and VR use cases in museums suggest that museums in future will be no stranger to the disruptive influence of digital technologies.

There are already many applications for museums, from virtual tour guides to whole exhibitions in VR.

Some may hesitate, arguing that this technological revolution means a surrender of culture to tech.

But we think the implementation of VR and AR in museums is not about replacing old traditions but about complementing them with more information and interactivity, to attract more people.

It is clear that the future of museums lies in the field of immersive technologies. To stay relevant and cater to the desires of technophile audiences, museums should embrace AR/VR and what these technologies have to offer.

Frequently Asked Questions

### What are the best examples of museums using AR?

There are museums that use AR to its full extent. For example:

*   Muséum national d’Histoire naturelle in Paris launched an AR exhibition about extinct animals;
*   The National Gallery in London put AR paintings outside the museums so that members of the public could access them via their phones;
*   The Art Gallery of Ontario, Toronto, launched an AR exhibition by Alex Mayhew that reimagines existing pieces in their collection.

### What are the best examples of museums using VR?

VR experiences are extremely popular across museums of any scale. For example:

*   London’s V&A launched a VR exhibition where visitors explore _Alice in Wonderland_ from the inside—they are put inside a simulation of Carroll’s world;
*   The Louvre has an impressive and precise VR simulation of the Mona Lisa;
*   The National Museum of Finland has a VR simulation that is a 3D version of the painting _The Opening of the Diet 1863 by Alexander II_.

The cost to develop an AR/VR app for a museum depends on the requirements of the app and development process, which include:

*   The size and experience of the development team;

*   Level of interactivity of the experience;

Advertisement

Exploring the impact of virtual reality on museum experiences: visitor immersion and experience consequences

You have full access to this open access article

64 Accesses

1 Altmetric

Explore all metrics

Abstract

Virtual Reality (VR) is used in cultural heritage to create immersive experiences that enhance visitor’s engagement. Mobile-VR provides a semi-immersive experience, while wearable-VR offers multi-modal interaction for a fully-immersive experience. This study aims to develop mobile-VR and wearable-VR based virtual frameworks for the Le-Corbusier Museum of Chandigarh, India, to measure the impact of VR on visitors’ experiences. This research evaluated immersion levels and motion sickness by comparing participants' experiences and analyzed the effectiveness of the VR systems in the museum's virtual environment. A total of 80 participants took part in the research, divided into two equal groups. They experienced VR tours on mobile and wearable devices. Immersion Tendency Questionnaire (ITQ) used to measure their immersion level and the Simulator Sickness Questionnaire (SSQ) used to evaluate their motion sickness after the VR experience. Based on the data collected from the participants, the user experience was assessed, and the effectiveness of the designed VR framework was evaluated using statistical analysis. The results identified a statistically significant difference between the designed VR systems in user experiences, as evaluated using the t-test (F = 3.49, df = 78.0, p < 0.001) for experience consequences. The study found that motion sickness was significantly reduced in wearable-VR compared to mobile-VR experiences. Additionally, there was a significant impact on immersion scores (F = − 3.39, df = 78.0, p < 0.001) when comparing the virtual tours of mobile-VR and wearable-VR. The evaluated results indicated that the wearable-VR system more effectively immerses the audience. These findings suggest that the wearable-VR is better at reducing motion sickness and improving immersion than mobile-VR.

1 Introduction

In recent years, integrating metaverse, wearable technology, Augmented Reality (AR), VR, and mixed reality technology in the museum sector has revolutionized how visitors engage with cultural and historical exhibits (Gao et al. 2021). Among the most prominent VR implementations are mobile-VR and wearable-VR tours, which offer immersive and interactive experience that can enhance visitor satisfaction and educational outcomes (Castagnetti et al. 2017). Mobile-VR provides limited interaction and semi-immersive experience to the participants. Xi Pei et al. researched the Museum of China, where participants experienced the museum tour using mobile-VR and desktop-VR. A mobile-VR participant suffered from motion sickness compared to other groups (Pei et al. 2023). Wearable-VR systems provide tracking, sensory feedback, and multi-modal interaction with a fully immersive experience that the participants can access using Oculus-VR, HTC Vive, and Meta-Quest VR headset. Jia Li et al. developed a wearable-VR system, and the results indicated that the designed system provides an interactive and immersive experience, and participants want to visit the museum in person after a VR tour (Li and Lv 2024). Quality of experience in a virtual tour refers to a participant's overall satisfaction and perception of a VR application, particularly in meeting their expectations. It was measured by technical performance like interactivity, content quality, and the user's emotional and cognitive engagement with the virtual experience (De Luca et al. 2023). Current research aims to evaluate the immersion level and user experiences during the virtual tour of the museum using of developed two types of VR systems mobile-VR and wearable-VR. By comparing the impact of mobile-VR, which utilizes smartphones and VR headsets, with wearable-VR, which involves more advanced, fully immersive equipment, this research seeks to determine which technology offers a superior visitor experience (Sylaiou et al. 2010). User engagement, learning effectiveness, emotional response, and overall satisfaction will be evaluated to understand the benefits and potential limitations of each VR approach (Brondi et al. 2016). The findings will offer valuable insights for museum curators and educators offering to optimize visitor interactions through innovative technological solutions.

Virtual reality is a technology that immerses people in a virtual world, making it difficult to focus on the real world. Improving the reality, the sense of realism, and the user's involvement is essential, making VR effective in cultural heritage, heritage education, and training (Augello et al. 2021). Mobile-VR, or smartphone-based VR, is much less immersive than wearable-VR headsets due to several key differences. The widescreen displays of wearable-VR headsets reduce edge-of-world effects and offer a more realistic field of view than traditional monitors or mobile devices (Rafeiro et al. 2024). They also incorporate advanced head and positional tracking (6DoF) (Bonatto et al. 2021), which allows users to move around within the virtual environment than mobile-VR, which is restricted to rotational movements (3DoF) (Papaefthymiou et al. 2015). Wearable-VR devices also have higher resolution displays and refresh rates, which help reduce motion blur and improve latency to present a smoother and more realistic picture. Immersion determines the extent of the user's feeling of'being there' in a virtual environment (Christopoulos et al. 2024). The greater the level of immersion, the more realistic and practical the experience becomes. Since VR is applied to cultural heritage to recreate real places, it is crucial to achieve high immersion to make the experience meaningful for users (Subramanian et al., 2020). Furthermore, motion controllers and haptic feedback provide a higher level of interaction to the user, thus making the user interact more with the environment. Mobile-VR relies on fundamental touch interactions and external controllers for control (Othman et al. 2022). Furthermore, dedicated processing power in standalone or PC-tethered headsets is better than mobile-VR in terms of graphical fidelity and responsiveness (Anastasovitis et al. 2024) due to the use of smartphone hardware. All these factors combine to make wearable-VR the preferred choice for highly immersive experiences for the user in simulations of cultural heritage and the preservation of heritage sites (Innocente et al., 2023). Virtual museums by VR enable users to visit virtual realms and explore and engage with cultural heritage assets with the help of wearable technologies (Alabau et al. 2024) (Buragohain et al. 2024). Research on immersion in a virtual environment presented that 360-degree VR video enhances visual immersion through visual quality assessment (Vlahovic et al. 2022). These technologies close the gap between the physical and virtual worlds and improve users' satisfaction with the virtual museum (Lee et al. 2020). Also, VR makes sharing cultural heritage with an international audience easy. People with no physical, geographical, or financial opportunity to visit a site can experience it using wearable technology, closing the access divide and increasing the accessibility of cultural heritage sites and objects (Recupero et al. 2019). One additional advantage of VR is its pedagogical value. VR enables students to actively participate in learning by experiencing virtual reconstructions of historical events. They can interact with artifacts and engage in role-playing scenarios, which helps them understand historical contexts more effectively. (Masduki et al. 2021). This higher level of participation may increase awareness and involvement in the conservation of cultural heritage (Gronowski et al. 2024).

Furthermore, this research also examined the effects of the user’s experience and motion sickness among the participants who used mobile-VR and wearable-VR devices (Hulusic et al. 2023). Wearable-VR has a better effect in reducing motion sickness and causing the least discomfort when using a high frame rate display, hand-tracking interactions, and better visual quality (Bekele et al., 2019). Motion sickness in VR, also called cyber sickness, is a condition that occurs when the visual sensors in the head receive information that is inconsistent with the information received by the body about the motion (Mimnaugh et al. 2023). This sensory mismatch can cause dizziness, nausea, headache, and disorientation, reducing user comfort and the effectiveness of VR applications in cultural heritage (Xiaoxiao et al. 2025). Motion sickness is a big problem in VR because, if not addressed, it can disengage the user and retard the uptake of VR applications in cultural heritage (Somrak et al. 2021). While building a mutual environment for the prototype of the application, the previous extensive research on wearable-VR with an individual story told a new and innovative story of the VR experience (Harley 2024). Formerly, virtual immersive media, such as graphics developed through the HCI process and actual photographic collections, was employed to mimic the history of culture and was afterward presented through desktop and mobile applications (Lim et al. 2019). However, there are still some issues concerning the conception and creation output of VR in the cultural heritage environment (Huggett 2020). In wearable-VR, the refresh rates of 90 Hz or more and the low latency reduce the motion blur that causes discomfort by rendering smooth motion transitions (Sangamuang et al. 2025). Also, 6DoF tracking allows the user to move around naturally within the virtual world, how one wants to move, and what one sees happening in the virtual world when one moves. Mobile-VR, on the other hand, restricts users to 3DoF, increasing the sensory discrepancy between the user’s body experiences and what they see (Somrak et al. 2021). These factors make wearable-VR more comfortable and immersive, thus reducing the incidence of motion sickness and enhancing the overall VR application experience. This is because these devices are easy to wear and can be incorporated into daily life. However, with these come new challenges that have been revealed, as well as the ones that are still present and which require further refinement (Corrales-Serrano et al. 2024) (Tsita et al. 2023) (Bianconi et al. 2023). The main issue for museum studies and digital technology is to shift the research interest to the perception from the content and technical perspective (Zheleva et al. 2024). This approach aims to decrease the feeling of motion sickness, increase the user’s comfort and satisfaction, and, in the end, improve the visitor’s experience in digital spaces with the help of virtual reality (Qiu et al. 2024).

This research aims to develop two VR frameworks, a mobile-based virtual tour and a wearable-VR virtual tour of the Museum of India, using dual-channel techniques, audio, textual information, and layered interaction with virtual artifacts to improve visitor engagement. This work empirically investigates the usage of advanced graphics technologies and multi-layered simulations in VR platforms. Beyond simple artifacts and visuals, the creators of this work have accomplished complicated asset interactions and dynamic material reactions to lighting changes. Apart from that, the designed system is upgradable, and we can replace and remove specific artifacts in the virtual museum (O’Meara et al. 2024). The developed system also provides an actual image of the artifact to compare the accuracy of the virtual 3D models of the artifacts. Still, all the features were missing in existing research. The current study also evaluated the motion sickness faced by the participants during a virtual tour of the museum using mobile-VR and wearable-VR systems because, in existing research, significantly fewer studies evaluated motion sickness, but this experience consequence was the main problem faced by the users during the VR tour.

The Le-Corbusier Museum, located in Chandigarh, India, is dedicated to the works of the great Swiss-French architect Le-Corbusier, who designed the city of Chandigarh in the 1950s (Rodríguez-Lora et al. 2021). Chandigarh is one of the few planned cities in India, and it reflects Le-Corbusier's creativity in modern urban planning and architecture, as shown in Fig. 1. The museum, housed in Le-Corbusier's old office, showcases his architectural drawings, models, furniture designs, and personal artifacts, offering insights into his innovative design philosophy (Sendai et al. 2017). This research used VR techniques to preserve and promote the heritage of India and can increase the number of visitors using VR tours of the museum.

a Exterior of Le-Corbusier museum, b Interior of museum with artifacts, c A panoramic collage of open hand monument, d Manhole Map (Map of Chandigarh City)

Research questions focus on specific aspects of the user experience immersion, experience consequences and critical responses for evaluating the consequences of users using VR technologies (Suhaeni et al. 2022). User’s immersion refers to the depth of engagement and sensory involvement a visitor experiences when interacting with a virtual or physical environment, such as a museum exhibit. In VR applications, immersion was often used to describe "being there," where visitors feel fully absorbed in the experience, as though they are in the virtual environment. Here, experience consequences mean the effects of user's interaction with a VR system, particularly in the user experience impacts their perceptions, emotions, behavior and performance after the museum's virtual tour (Mills et al. 2024). This study carried out nine parameters of the consequences parameter, including fatigue, motion sickness, dizziness, and emotional discomfort, frustration, and eye strain. Using these parameters, researchers can evaluate the impact of designed VR systems on participants' well-being, ensuring that the system is comfortable and minimizes negative physical consequences, which is crucial for improving overall user experience. This study addressed the following research questions.

RQ1: How do mobile-VR and wearable-VR museum tours of cultural heritages differ in their impact on user immersion?

RQ2: How do mobile-VR and wearable-VR tours of museums influence user experience consequence response to their knowledge?

This study evaluates the immersion and experience consequences of mobile-VR and wearable-VR tours of the museum on the user's experiences. By comparing these two types of VR systems, to determine which VR technology offers a superior user experience (Spallone et al. 2024). Section 1 describes the introduction, and Sect. 2 contains related work in the field of cultural heritages. It also explains the user interface with virtual tours. Sections 3 and 4 describe the methodology of 3D modeling and the research, which contains the complete process of VR development, experimental process, data collection, measurement tools, and participant details. Section 5 evaluated the results and discussion based on the user's feedback. Section 6 describes the conclusion of the research work.

2 Literature review: VR in cultural heritage

The use of VR technology in cultural heritage sites and museums has recently become a significant topic. Researchers have investigated the impact of VR on visitors' experience, engagement, and learning outcomes. It is possible to state that VR plays a key role in the conservation, exposition, and marketing of cultural heritage. Through immersive and interactive experiences, VR technology changes how audiences engage with historical sites, artifacts, and traditions (Okanovic et al. 2022). In preserving and restoring cultural heritage sites, VR is used to create detailed 3D models of deteriorating structures (Monaco et al. 2022), which helps digital archiving and future restoration. For instance, virtual reconstructions of historical monuments can be a reference for physical restoration projects (Clini et al. 2017). Similarly, integrating adaptive AI-driven systems could provide more personalized and immersive experiences and be compatible with emerging technologies that will make VR devices for future generation (Rodriguez-Garcia et al. 2024). With the growing advancement of technology, the VR system has evolved with mobile technologies combined with specially designed wearable technologies to provide consumers with a real VR experience (Chong et al. 2021). The development of mobile technologies such as smartphones and tablets as VR platforms has been enhanced by improvements in mobile intelligence and performance requirements (Singh et al., 2019). Today's VR technologies, which include HMDs, affect various participants and come in different types of devices (Angra et al. 2022). First, the limitations of the previous system must be thoroughly reviewed and addressed, especially in light of the rapid pace of technological advancement (Ambrosio et al., 2020). In order to achieve the full potential of VR, it is necessary to focus on enhancing the technical aspects of VR and its features. This may include enhancing the resolution and responsiveness of the display device, the accuracy and sensitivity of motion tracking, and the haptic feedback mechanisms to make the sensations more realistic (Chong et al. 2020).

Furthermore, VR enhances visitor experiences by allowing users to investigate heritages and artifacts in ways impossible in the real world (Chen and Hua 2024). Visitors can virtually walk through ancient buildings, view artifacts from multiple angles, and experience historical events as if they were there (Datta et al. 2024), deepening their understanding and appreciation of cultural heritage (Spallone et al. 2023). As an educational tool, VR offers unique, hands-on learning experiences. Virtual tours and simulations can be integrated into school curricula, providing learners with a comprehensive understanding of historical contexts and cultural significance (Roumana et al. 2022). Table 1 illustrates the existing research on mobile-VR and wearable-VR conducted in the different countries' heritages.

VR is an effective way of promoting cultural heritage and increasing tourism. It is possible to use virtual tours to show potential visitors what they can expect and thus encourage more people to visit in person (Tsita et al. 2023). VR experiences can also be posted on social media and other platforms, which increase awareness and interest in cultural heritage sites (Paulauskas et al. 2023). Researchers also use VR to document and analyze cultural heritage sites. High-resolution 3D models and virtual reconstructions can be used for detailed study and close examination without putting the actual site or artifact at risk and for research, planning, and collaboration between experts from other disciplines (Spallone et al. 2024). This study aims to determine the effectiveness of different types of VR frameworks and assess how the designed systems affect the participants' immersion level and motion sickness. This research includes a comparison between mobile VR and wearable VR. This study examines the impact of different types of VR frameworks and compares the developed systems' effectiveness in terms of immersion and motion sickness effects on the participants. This research includes a comparison between mobile-VR and wearable-VR. This study overcomes all existing problems, compares the platform dependability in cultural heritage sites, and evaluates the system's effectiveness based on the participants' feedback (Kim et al. 2018). Additionally, the researcher reveals that motion sickness was a central problem faced by the users during VR museum tours (Iacono et al. 2024); this research mainly focused on the design and development of immersive and interactive VR systems for the Museum of Chandigarh using high-resolution visual frameworks, 6DOF moments, and above 90HZ frame rate to enhance the immersion and to overcome the motion sickness of the users during VR museum. Two VR applications were developed: mobile-VR and wearable-VR systems. The impact of both VR systems was evaluated in this research using an immersion tendency questionnaire to evaluate the immersion and users' experiences with satisfaction using both VR platforms. This research also used simulator sickness questionnaires to assess motion sickness and its consequences. We compared the VR systems based on the level of motion sickness of the participants based on the VR tour of the museum.

3 VR framework development

This section explains the complete workflow of the designed systems, including the research design approach, 3D modeling process, development of VR systems and artifacts for the museum, and full design and development process of VR applications. The main focus is on the development of two VR frameworks: Mobile and wearable-VR museum. Apart from that, this section also describes the background of the museum. The Le-Corbusier Museum in Chandigarh is a tribute to the renowned architect Le-Corbusier, who pivotally designed the city. Le Corbusier, a Swiss-French architect, was commissioned in 1950 to bring this vision to life. The Le-Corbusier Museum, also known as the Le-Corbusier Centre, is situated in Sector 19, Chandigarh (India). It houses a collection of his sketches, drawings, and personal belongings, providing insights into his creative process and vision for the city. The exhibits include models of the city's buildings, photographs documenting the construction process, and multimedia presentations on Le-Corbusier's life and work (Engineering 2018). This museum is an educational and cultural hub, attracting architects, students, and tourists worldwide (Hadjipanayi et al. 2024). The current study focused on developing a virtual tour of this museum using virtual reality technology to evaluate the impact of the VR framework on visitors. This research aims to assess the user experience in the virtual museum of Chandigarh that provides participants immersion and reduces motion sickness during the experience.

3.1 Research design approach

The research design process explains the complete task done by the researcher during this study. Five tasks were performed in this research. Task 1 involved defining the aim of the research and selecting a case study to design a VR system. Le-Corbusier Museum of Chandigarh was chosen as a case study (Angra 2022). After that, we must create the 3D models to develop the VR system. Task 2 defines the complete design and development strategies, including resources like Blender, Unity 3D, APIs, and VR headsets (Gabajová and Kraj 2021). Tasks 3 and 4 defined the complete pre-processing and post-processing instructions, including the experimental process after completing the development process, as shown in Fig. 2. The participants visited the virtual tour of both applications and evaluated their effectiveness based on the visitors' feedback.

Research design process

3.2 3D modeling of artifacts

3D modeling was done using the modeling software Blender and Maya. Before modeling, pre-processing steps were taken to gather data from the Museum of Le Corbusier using a DSLR camera and a mobile phone (Ivashchuk 2024). Different types of artifacts, paintings, and drawings are available in these museums. Based on the collected data, 3D modeling was performed, and 3D models of exterior, interior, and artifacts were designed using Blender and Maya (Ivashchuk et al. 2024). The modeling phase involves using polygonal modeling or digital sculpting to create the object's structure. Texturing follows, involving UV mapping and applying textures for details. Rigging adds a skeleton for movement, and animation defines the model's motion (Iizuka et al. 2022). The rendering stage involves setting up lighting and generating the final image or animation. Finally, post-processing includes compositing and editing to refine the output. Figure 3 illustrates the complete process of 3D modeling, using texturing, including the virtual artifacts and exterior design of the Le-Corbusier Museum. All these 3D models were used to develop the museum's virtual tour in Unity, explained in detail as shown in Figs. 4, 5, and 6.

3D modeling process

Wooden chair (artifact of Le-Corbusier Museum, India) 3D model without texturing

Wooden chair 3D models with textures

3D models of artifacts and exterior design of the museum

3.3 Virtual museum development of mobile-VR and wearable-VR framework

After completing the 3D modeling of artifacts and the museum's exterior, the next step is to design the virtual environment using Unity 3D (Donghui et al. 2019). Setting up the development environment required the installation of Unity3D and Android build support because the Oculus Quest application runs on the Android build module. Two frameworks were developed for mobile-VR, via Unity version 2019.4.34f1, and the second for a wearable-VR tour of the museum, using Unity version 2020. 3.48f1 (Roumana et al. 2022).

Unity 3D offers a user-friendly development environment, extensive VR support and integration with major VR hardware like Oculus Rift, HTC Vive, and PlayStation VR. Unity provides tools for creating immersive 3D environments, realistic physics, detailed graphics, and a comprehensive asset store for additional resources. The scripting in C# and strong community support makes it an ideal choice for efficiently creating high-quality, interactive VR experiences (Banfi et al. 2023). A current study developed two Virtual tours, mobile-based VR runs using IRUS Play station and wearable-VR tour experience using an Oculus Quest-1 VR headset, as shown in the next section.

3.3.1 Wearable-VR framework

Developing the second version of the wearable-VR framework used Unity 2020, Oculus VR SDK, and Oculus Quest-1 VR headset to design a virtual environment, which includes four interactive UI VR scenes, as shown in Fig. 7a. Figure 7 illustrates the complete development process and implementation of the designed wearable-VR tour of the Museum of Chandigarh. To develop a fully immersive virtual tour for a museum using Unity 3D for Oculus VR headset, first of all, set up the development environment by installing Unity and the Oculus Integration package and configure Unity for VR by enabling Oculus support in the XR Plug-in Management settings (Singh and Ahmad 2024). Now import all designed 3D assets and create the virtual museum environment, adding lighting, navigation paths, and interactive elements as shown in Fig. 7a. Developed wearable-VR system including the exterior, interior, option UI, and exit UI scene to interact with virtual artifacts and access information about the museum. Wearable-VR tour of Le-Corbusier provides dual-channel interaction; audio and textual data can be accessed using controllers. Implement VR interactions using Oculus prefabs, such as OVRCameraRig, and set up hand controllers for user interactions (Jangra et al. 2023). Test the VR framework using Unity’s Play Mode with the Oculus headset connected and optimize performance by adjusting settings and optimizing assets (Gong et al. 2024). Finally, configure the build settings, build the application, and deploy it to the Oculus headset for installation. Conduct user testing to gather feedback and refine the application based on user experience. At the end, visitors can experience an interactive and immersive virtual tour of the Le-Corbusier museum using an Oculus VR headset, where users can interact with virtual artifacts as well as actual images of the museums as shown in Fig. 7f by clicking explore and gallery UI icons during virtual tour. During a mobile-VR tour, visitors cannot move freely in 360-degree, and the controller and multiple interactions are missing; that’s why it provides a semi-immersive experience, but wearable-VR provides a fully immersive experience where participants can freely move in 6DOF and engages the audience in an interactive way (Alabau et al. 2024).

a Main page of virtual tour in Oculus-VR, b View of virtual reality museum with all the artifacts in Oculus-VR system. c Virtual artifacts of museum with UI interaction, d Interaction with dual-channel (textual & audio) using explore and gallery icon. e Interact with artifact of Harappan findings with textual & audio details, f Interact with real image of artifact using gallery icon

Figure 7 illustrates the complete development process of the museum's virtual tour in the Oculus VR headset. The participants performed five tasks during the virtual tour to enjoy and explore the full information about the museum's history virtually using VR technology. The tasks performed by participants are mentioned below.

Task 1: The researcher provided all the basic instructions on navigating the Oculus VR headset and controllers. Navigate the Oculus Home or App Library and Locate and launch the VR tour app to experience the museum's virtual tour. Then, participants can access the welcome UI with four interactive UI buttons: explore exterior, options, and exit, as shown in Fig. 7a. Click on the exterior to experience the virtual model of the museum building.

Task 2: Participants can use controller navigation to visit the museum's 3D model. If they can move to the next virtual room of the museum, they can click on the explore UI button and enter the virtual room of the museum.

Task 3: The museum's interior design is display

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

enriched_points.expanded_content: Virtual Reality (VR) and Augmented Reality (AR) enhance visitor engagement by creating immersive experiences, allowing exploration of exhibits remotely and enriching understanding of artifacts.

Artificial Intelligence (AI) is increasingly utilized in museums for personalized visitor experiences, optimizing collections management, and creating interactive educational content that caters to diverse audiences.

Digital archiving enhances accessibility, enabling global audiences to explore vast collections online, preserving cultural heritage while ensuring materials are accessible to researchers and enthusiasts.
3D scanning and modeling technology allows for detailed digital reproductions of artifacts, facilitating virtual exhibitions and interactive displays that foster deeper educational engagement.
